metrics.name: Hormuz
metrics.question: Will daily oil tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more on any day between April 29 and June 1, 2026?

provenance.quantity: daily oil tanker transits through Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more
provenance.fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

notes: no leakage-audited reliability combiner is fitted — prior and simulation are reported separately with the feasible combined range; no fixed blend is applied and Lean V2 must not become default on this basis

simulation_result.question: Will daily oil tanker transits through the Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more on any day between April 29 and June 1, 2026?
limitations: forecast decomposition — grounded prior 0.5 (counted n=2); simulation-conditional 0.125 (resolved mass 1.00); headline 0.125 via combiner_unavailable_range_only. Prior and simulation are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule.
weight_sensitive: within the counted reference-class intervals P(YES) spans [0.0, 1.0]
bounded omitted-state residual 0.314: the interval widens to [0.0, 1.0] — private-state omissions are BOUNDS, never unknown-state worlds

no leakage-audited prior↔simulation reliability combiner is fitted — the headline is the mass-based recovery blend (resolved mass keeps its simulated conditional; unresolved mass takes the grounded prior); the two inputs are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule

blueprint.causal_thesis: The Strait remains effectively closed due to Iranian mining and US blockade since late February 2026. Resumption to 50 tankers/day requires coordinated de-escalation: Iran clearing mines and lifting closure, US lifting blockade, and tanker operators resuming transit. Without such actions, traffic st

pruned.reason: ceremonial: no discretion over any surviving terminal pathway

hormuz_closure_regime.claim: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to commercial oil tanker traffic due to military conflict and blockade.

provenance.definition: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to commercial oil tanker traffic due to military conflict and blockade.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Iran shut the strait and mined the waterway after U.S.-Israeli strikes killed Supreme Leader Khamenei on February 28, 2026.
cases.basis_quote: Iran shut the strait and reportedly mined parts of the waterway; the U.S. subsequently imposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports.

cases.description: As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.
cases.basis_quote: As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.

pre_conflict_traffic_baseline.claim: Pre-conflict daily oil tanker traffic through the strait was approximately 100 tankers per day (half of 125-140 total vessels).

provenance.definition: Pre-conflict daily oil tanker traffic through the strait was approximately 100 tankers per day (half of 125-140 total vessels).
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed daily, with roughly half being oil tankers.
cases.basis_quote: Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed in and out of the strait daily.

outcome_reference_class.quantity: daily oil tanker transits through Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more

provenance.definition: At least 50 commercial oil tankers transit the strait in a single UTC day between April 29 and June 1, 2026.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Reported by Reuters, Lloyd's List, or Bloomberg as reaching or exceeding 50 tankers in a day.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Reports of total vessel traffic (including non-tankers) reaching 50; reports from other sources.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Pre-conflict, daily oil tanker traffic was approximately 100, well above 50.
cases.basis_quote: Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed daily, with roughly half being oil tankers.

cases.description: On April 27, 2026, zero oil tankers transited, far below 50.
cases.basis_quote: As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.

weight_invariant.rule: every weight traces to a counted reference class or is explicit unknown mass; no qualitative label is mapped to a number

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

iranian_supreme_leader.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['iran_clear_mines'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

us_naval_commander.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['us_lift_blockade'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

tanker_operators.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['tankers_transit'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

iranian_supreme_leader.claim: The strait is a strategic asset; I will keep it closed and mined to inflict maximum economic pain on the US and its allies, even if it devastates Iran's own economy. I view any tanker transit as a violation of sovereignty and will order IRGC to fire on any vessel attempting passage.
beliefs: Closure forces US to negotiate
Mines are effective deterrent
Tanker operators will not risk attack
The strait is a strategic asset;
I will keep it closed and mined to inflict maximum economic pain on the US and its allies, even if it devastates Iran's own economy.
I view any tanker transit as a violation of sovereignty and will order IRGC to fire on any vessel attempting passage.
Domestic hardliners demand continued closure;
IRGC Quds Force advocates escalation
commitments: Keep strait closed until sanctions lifted
iranian_supreme_leader.pressures: Domestic hardliners demand continued closure; IRGC Quds Force advocates escalation
supporting_evidence_ids: Iran shut the strait and reportedly mined parts of the waterway
historical_case_refs: Iran-Iraq War tanker war (1984-1988) where Iran mined strait and attacked shipping
distinguishing_observations: No tankers transit; IRGC boats active near mines; diplomatic channels silent
iranian_supreme_leader.action_if_state: Order IRGC to maintain minefields, deploy speedboats to harass any tanker approaching strait, and publicly threaten to sink vessels that attempt transit without permission.
assumptions: US will not escalate to direct strikes on Iranian soil
latent_beliefs: Closure forces US to negotiate
Mines are effective deterrent
Tanker operators will not risk attack
The strait is a strategic asset;
I will keep it closed and mined to inflict maximum economic pain on the US and its allies, even if it devastates Iran's own economy.
I view any tanker transit as a violation of sovereignty and will order IRGC to fire on any vessel attempting passage.
Domestic hardliners demand continued closure;
IRGC Quds Force advocates escalation
known_commitments: Keep strait closed until sanctions lifted

iranian_supreme_leader.claim: I can selectively allow tanker transits to signal flexibility or to reward allies, while keeping the strait nominally closed. I will permit a few Iranian-flagged tankers to pass to test US blockade, but will not allow 50 per day.
beliefs: Partial opening can split US coalition
Tanker operators will follow signals
I can selectively allow tanker transits to signal flexibility or to reward allies, while keeping the strait nominally closed.
I will permit a few Iranian-flagged tankers to pass to test US blockade, but will not allow 50 per day.
Economic hardship from oil revenue loss;
moderate faction urges limited opening
commitments: Retaliate if US attacks permitted vessels
iranian_supreme_leader.pressures: Economic hardship from oil revenue loss; moderate faction urges limited opening
supporting_evidence_ids: Iran oil tankers turned back by US blockade
historical_case_refs: Iran's 'oil for goods' swaps during sanctions (2012-2015)
distinguishing_observations: Occasional tanker transits under IRGC escort; mixed signals from Tehran
iranian_supreme_leader.action_if_state: Secretly clear a narrow channel, allow 1-2 Iranian tankers per week to transit, and signal to foreign tankers that they may pass if they pay a fee or carry Iranian oil.
assumptions: US will not sink Iranian tankers
Tanker operators will accept risk for profit
latent_beliefs: Partial opening can split US coalition
Tanker operators will follow signals
I can selectively allow tanker transits to signal flexibility or to reward allies, while keeping the strait nominally closed.
I will permit a few Iranian-flagged tankers to pass to test US blockade, but will not allow 50 per day.

moderate faction urges limited opening
known_commitments: Retaliate if US attacks permitted vessels

us_naval_commander.claim: My orders are to enforce a total blockade of Iranian ports and prevent any oil tanker from transiting the strait. I will board, divert, or disable any vessel attempting to carry Iranian oil, and I will not allow any tanker to pass until Iran agrees to demilitarize the strait.
beliefs: Blockade is legal under self-defense
Tanker operators will comply if enforcement is credible
Mines are a secondary threat
My orders are to enforce a total blockade of Iranian ports and prevent any oil tanker from transiting the strait.
I will board, divert, or disable any vessel attempting to carry Iranian oil, and I will not allow any tanker to pass until Iran agrees to demilitarize the strait.
Pentagon demands results;
allies in region want stability;
risk of escalation with Iran
commitments: Zero tolerance for Iranian oil shipments
Use minimum force but escalate if needed
us_naval_commander.pressures: Pentagon demands results; allies in region want stability; risk of escalation with Iran
supporting_evidence_ids: US subsequently imposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports
Iran oil tankers turned back by US blockade
historical_case_refs: US blockade of Iraq (1990-2003) - maritime interception operations
distinguishing_observations: US Navy vessels stop and inspect all vessels near strait; no tankers pass
us_naval_commander.action_if_state: Maintain continuous patrols, board any tanker approaching strait, divert non-compliant vessels to Bahrain for inspection, and fire warning shots if necessary.
assumptions: Iran will not attack US warships directly
Tanker operators will not test blockade
latent_beliefs: Blockade is legal under self-defense
Tanker operators will comply if enforcement is credible

us_naval_commander.claim: I have discretion to allow non-Iranian tankers to transit if they prove they are not carrying Iranian oil. I will focus on interdicting Iranian-flagged vessels and those suspected of sanctions evasion, but will let others pass to avoid international backlash.
beliefs: Total blockade is unsustainable politically
Risk of collision or incident is high
Tanker operators will cooperate if rules are clear
I have discretion to allow non-Iranian tankers to transit if they prove they are not carrying Iranian oil.
I will focus on interdicting Iranian-flagged vessels and those suspected of sanctions evasion, but will let others pass to avoid international backlash.
State Department wants to avoid alienating allies;
CENTCOM worries about force protection

historical_case_refs: US maritime interception operations in Persian Gulf (1987-1988) - tanker reflagging
distinguishing_observations: Some tankers pass after inspection; US Navy sets up a 'safe corridor' for non-Iranian vessels

assumptions: Iran will not attack transiting tankers

tanker_operators.claim: The strait is too dangerous due to mines and military confrontation. We will not send any tankers through until there is a clear, internationally guaranteed safe passage agreement. The risk of being sunk or seized outweighs any profit.
beliefs: Mines are unpredictable
Insurance will not cover war risk
Crews refuse to sail
The strait is too dangerous due to mines and military confrontation.
We will not send any tankers through until there is a clear, internationally guaranteed safe passage agreement.
The risk of being sunk or seized outweighs any profit.
Insurance premiums skyrocket;
charterers cancel contracts;
crew unions protest
commitments: No transit until safe passage declared
tanker_operators.pressures: Insurance premiums skyrocket; charterers cancel contracts; crew unions protest
supporting_evidence_ids: only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers
historical_case_refs: Tanker operators avoided Strait of Hormuz during Iran-Iraq tanker war until reflagging (1987)
distinguishing_observations: Tanker traffic near zero; vessels loiter outside strait; no new bookings for Hormuz transit
tanker_operators.action_if_state: Keep all tankers outside the strait, reroute around Africa or via pipeline, and refuse any charter that requires strait transit.
latent_beliefs: Mines are unpredictable
Insurance will not cover war risk
Crews refuse to sail
The strait is too dangerous due to mines and military confrontation.
We will not send any tankers through until there is a clear, internationally guaranteed safe passage agreement.
The risk of being sunk or seized outweighs any profit.
Insurance premiums skyrocket;
charterers cancel contracts;
crew unions protest
known_commitments: No transit until safe passage declared

tanker_operators.claim: We see a window to transit if we can secure insurance, pay for naval escort, or disguise cargo. Some operators will risk it for high profits, especially if US enforcement is selective. We will send tankers through if we believe we can pass undetected or with permission.
beliefs: US blockade is porous
Iran may allow passage for a fee
First movers will earn premium
We see a window to transit if we can secure insurance, pay for naval escort, or disguise cargo.
Some operators will risk it for high profits, especially if US enforcement is selective.
We will send tankers through if we believe we can pass undetected or with permission.
Charterers offer high rates;
competitors may act;
owners demand utilization
tanker_operators.pressures: Charterers offer high rates; competitors may act; owners demand utilization
supporting_evidence_ids: pre-conflict total vessel traffic was 125–140 ships/day
historical_case_refs: Tanker operators used flags of convenience and false documentation during Iran sanctions (2010-2015)
distinguishing_observations: A few tankers attempt transit; some are turned back, some succeed; insurance rates vary

Iran will not attack tankers that pay

sample.role: Decides strait closure and mine clearance
latent_beliefs: Closure forces US to negotiate
Mines are effective deterrent
Tanker operators will not risk attack
The strait is a strategic asset;
I will keep it closed and mined to inflict maximum economic pain on the US and its allies, even if it devastates Iran's own economy.
I view any tanker transit as a violation of sovereignty and will order IRGC to fire on any vessel attempting passage.
Domestic hardliners demand continued closure;
IRGC Quds Force advocates escalation
public_facts: (reported) [2026-02-27] Pre-conflict, daily oil tanker traffic was approximately 100, well above 50. — “Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed daily, with roughly half being oil tankers.”

(reported) [2026-04-27] On April 27, 2026, zero oil tankers transited, far below 50. — “As of April 27, 2026, only about 7 ships transited the strait in the prior 24 hours, none of which were oil tankers carrying cargo for the global market.”
(confirmed) pre-conflict daily oil tanker traffic — “pre-conflict total vessel traffic was 125–140 ships/day”
(confirmed) current traffic reduction — “commercial crossings have fallen by approximately 94% from peacetime levels”

(confirmed) [2026-02-28] The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to commercial oil tanker traffic due to military conflict and blockade. — “Iran shut the strait and reportedly mined parts of the waterway; the U.S. subsequently imposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports.”

(confirmed) [2026-02-27] Pre-conflict daily oil tanker traffic through the strait was approximately 100 tankers per day (half of 125-140 total vessels). — “Pre-conflict, between 125 and 140 ships typically crossed in and out of the strait daily.”
commitments: Keep strait closed until sanctions lifted

checks.note: all terminal-writing effects are known mechanical kinds
at least one resolving path exists
numeric predicates need a bounded mechanism in question units

engine_primary.weight_source: counted_reference_class_posteriors (no qualitative label is mapped to a number anywhere in this engine); represented states carry the full branch mass — omitted-state residuals are bounded interval-wideners, never unknown-state worlds

skipped_reasons: no deterministic trigger fired: primary stable, no verified evidence conflict, no reversal-capable disputed assumption (imagination alone is not a trigger)

replicate_policy.reason: behavioral replicates not requested (deterministic execution: content-addressed draws make replicate 0 exhaustive)

provenance.quantity: daily oil tanker transits through Strait of Hormuz reach 50 or more
provenance.fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 2 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

notes: no leakage-audited reliability combiner is fitted — prior and simulation are reported separately with the feasible combined range; no fixed blend is applied and Lean V2 must not become default on this basis

consequences.boundary_contract: delivery mechanics precompiled; interpretation/persuasion/reaction remain branch-local actor decisions

iran_clear_mines.description: Iran orders mine clearance in strait

us_lift_blockade.description: US lifts naval blockade of Iranian ports

tankers_transit.description: Tanker operators send vessels through strait

novel_clear_mines_in_corridor.description: Iranian Supreme Leader orders selective clearance of naval mines in a designated corridor to allow limited Iranian-flagged tanker transits while maintaining nominal closure.

novel_define_documentation_requirements.description: Define clear documentation requirements for tankers to prove non-Iranian cargo, and communicate these rules to tanker operators and allies.

params.value: tankers must provide proof of non-Iranian cargo

novel_continue_aggressive_blockade.description: US naval commander continues aggressive enforcement of the blockade to prevent Iranian oil exports.

novel_transit_strait.description: Tanker operators physically navigate a vessel through a strategic strait, altering the vessel's geospatial state and potentially triggering maritime traffic monitoring events.

novel_send_test_tanker.description: Tanker operators dispatch a single test tanker through the strait with disguised cargo and a pre-arranged bribe to Iranian officials, while monitoring US naval patrol patterns.

novel_transit_strait_with_disguise_and_b.description: Tanker operators disguise vessel identity and bribe local officials to secure safe passage through the strait, capture high charter rates, and test US enforcement selectivity.
